root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

the algebraic theory III: vector bundles + locally free sheaves

Table of contents

  1. Locally trivial vector bundles
  2. Sheaves of modules
  3. Sections of vector bundles
  4. Constructing vector bundles
  5. A fundamental equivalence of categories

Prerequisites

Locally trivial vector bundles

We defined in an earlier post a set-theoretic vector bundle to be a surjective function

\begin{equation}\notag \pi: E\to B \end{equation}

of sets such that each fiber

\begin{equation}\notag E_b \defeq \pi^{-1}(b) \end{equation}

is an $\bbr$-vector space. If

\begin{equation}\notag \pi’:E’ \to B \end{equation}

is a second set-theoretic vector bundle, then a morphism of set-theoretic vector bundles from $\pi$ to $\pi’$ is a function $\theta: E\to E’$ such that the diagram

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{xy} \xymatrix{ \color{white}E \ar@[white][rr]^{\color{white}\theta} \ar@[white][dr]_{\color{white}\pi} & & \color{white} E’ \ar@[white][dl]^{\color{white}\pi’} \\ & \color{white}B} \end{xy} \end{equation}

commutes. We say $\theta$ is an isomorphism if it is bijective, in which case its inverse $\theta^{-1}:E’ \to E$ is easily seen to be a morphism of set-theoretic vector bundles.

Now, suppose that we are given a set-theoretic vector bundle $\pi:E \to B$. From the fibers of $\pi$ we may form the disjoint union

\begin{equation}\notag E’ = \bigsqcup_{b\in B} E_b, \end{equation}

and then let $\pi’: E’ \to B$ be the natural projection. Then there is an evident isomorphism of set-theoretic vector bundles $\theta: E \to E’$ which is given on fibers by

\begin{equation}\notag \theta_b: E_b \to E’_b = \{b\} \times E_b, \quad e \mapsto (b,e). \end{equation}

This shows that an equivalent way to conceptualize a set-theoretic vector bundle (up to isomorphism) is as an indexed (or parametrized) collection $\{E_b\}_{b\in B}$ of vector spaces.

In this post, we shall consider set-theoretic vector bundles in the category of smooth manifolds:

Definition. A smooth vector bundle is a set-theoretic vector bundle $\pi:E \to M$ where the sets $E$ and $M$ are both smooth manifolds, and with respect to these structures, $\pi$ is a smooth map. The spaces $E$ and $M$ are called the total and base spaces of the vector bundle.

A morphism of smooth vector bundles, denoted

\begin{equation}\notag \theta: (\pi:E\to M) \to (\pi’:E’\to M), \end{equation}

is a function $\theta:E\to E’$ which is both a morphism of the underlying set-theoretic vector bundles and is smooth. (Note that this a slight abuse of notation, by using the symbol $\theta$ twice.)

The identity morphism

\begin{equation}\notag \id_\pi: (\pi:E\to M) \to (\pi:E\to M) \end{equation}

of the smooth vector bundle $\pi$ is defined to be the morphism $\id_\pi = \id_E : E\to E$.

If

\begin{equation}\notag \theta’: (\pi:E’\to M) \to (\pi'':E''\to M) \end{equation}

is a second morphism of smooth vector bundles, the composition

\begin{equation}\notag \theta’ \circ \theta: (\pi:E\to M) \to (\pi'':E''\to M) \end{equation}

is defined to be the standard composition $\theta’ \circ \theta:E\to E’’$ of functions.

With these objects, morphisms, identity morphisms, and composition law, we obtain the category of smooth vector bundles over $M$, denoted $\Vect_M$.

As with set-theoretic vector bundles, we may conceptualize a smooth vector bundle $\pi:E \to M$ as an indexed collection $\{E_p\}_{p\in M}$ of vector spaces. This time, however, the indexing set is a smooth manifold, and thus we may conceptualize the collection $\{E_p\}_{p\in M}$ as a “smoothly parametrized” family of vector spaces. Here, “smoothness” of the parametrization is encoded in the smoothness of $E$, $M$ and $\pi$.

For example, we might consider the collection $\{T_p(M)\}_{p\in M}$ of all tangent spaces over a smooth manifold $M$. Intuitively, the vector spaces in this collection should “vary smoothly” with $p$. But as we saw in a previous post, the tangent bundle

\begin{equation}\notag T(M) \defeq \bigsqcup_{p\in M} T_p(M) \end{equation}

has a smooth manifold structure with respect to which the natural projection $\pi:T(M) \to M$ is smooth. The fiber

\begin{equation}\notag \pi^{-1}(p) = \{p\} \times T_p(M) \end{equation}

may be naturally identified with $T_p(M)$, and thus the family of vector spaces $\{T_p(M)\}_{p\in M}$ may be naturally identified with the smooth vector bundle $\pi:T(M) \to M$. This identification makes precise our intuition that the tangent space $T_p(M)$ should “vary smoothly” with $p$.

We may manufacture a smooth vector bundle over a smooth manifold $M$ by taking the natural projection

\begin{equation}\notag \pi_1: M \times \bbr^k \to M \end{equation}

onto the first cartesian factor. If $\pi:E \to M$ is a smooth vector bundle which is isomorphic to $\pi_1$ (in the category $\Vect_M$), then $\pi$ is called a trivial smooth vector bundle of rank $k$.

Not all smooth vector bundles are trivial; in fact, we saw in our earlier post on tangent bundles that the tangent bundle of the sphere $S^2$ is not trivial. However, if $M$ is any manifold, we also saw that there is an open covering $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$ of $M$ for which the restricted smooth vector bundle

\begin{equation}\notag \pi: \pi^{-1}(U_i) \to U_i \end{equation}

is trivial, as a smooth vector bundle over the open set $U_i$. Thus, while the tangent bundle $T(M)$ may not be globally trivial, it is always locally trivial in this sense.

The category of locally trivial smooth vector bundles will be one of the main focuses of this post. Let’s formally define this category:

Definition. A locally trivial smooth vector bundle of rank $k$ is a smooth vector bundle $\pi:E\to M$ for which there exists an open cover $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$ such that each restricted smooth vector bundle

\begin{equation}\notag \pi: \pi^{-1}(U_i) \to U_i \end{equation}

is isomorphic (in the category $\Vect_{U_i}$) to the trivial smooth vector bundle

\begin{equation}\notag \pi_1: U_i \times \bbr^k \to U_i. \end{equation}

In this case, the open cover $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$ is called a trivializing open cover of the vector bundle, the vector bundle is said to trivialize over $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$, and isomorphisms

\begin{equation}\notag \Phi_i: (U_i \times \bbr^k \to U_i) \to (\pi^{-1}(U_i) \to U_i) \end{equation}

of smooth vector bundles over $U_i$ are called (local) trivializations.

The category of locally trivial smooth vector bundles is the full subcategory of $\Vect_M$ whose objects are the locally trivial smooth vector bundles of rank $k$, for all $k$.

In many texts, locally trivial smooth vector bundles are the only types of vector bundles considered, and hence these objects are usually just called vector bundles. We shall follow the same practice:

Terminology and Notation. For the rest of this post, a vector bundle will be a locally trivial smooth vector bundle of rank $k$, for some $k$. A morphism of vector bundles will be a morphism of smooth vector bundles. In particular, the notation $\Vect_M$ will be re-defined to stand for the category of locally trivial smooth vector bundles over $M$.

Furthermore, when no confusion is likely to arise, we will reference a vector bundle $\pi:E \to M$ by its total space $E$ only. Accordingly, a morphism of vector bundles

\begin{equation}\notag \theta: (\pi:E \to M) \to (\pi’: E’\to M) \end{equation}

will be denoted only by the map $\theta:E \to E’$ on total spaces.

One of the most useful properties of morphisms of (locally trivial smooth!) vector bundles is that the isomorphism property may be tested locally on the fibers. Precisely:

Fiberwise Criterion for Vector Bundle Isomorphisms. Let $\theta: E \to E’$ be a morphism of vector bundles over a common base space $M$. Then $\theta$ is an isomorphism of vector bundles if and only if each induced map $\alpha_p:E_p \to E’_p$ on the fibers is a linear isomorphism.

I suggest that you attempt to prove this theorem on your own. You will see that the local triviality of the vector bundles is used in a crucial way.

Sheaves of modules

The main goal of this post is to describe a fundamental equivalence between the category $\Vect_M$ and a certain category of sheaves of modules. In this section, we define this second category. To prepare, I suggest that you review the definitions of locally ringed spaces and sheaves in the previous post.

While we defined sheaves in the previous post, we did not define morphisms of sheaves. We do so now, restricting to the case of sheaves of sets:

Definition. Let $\cale$ and $\cale’$ be sheaves of sets on a topological space $X$. A morphism of sheaves, denoted $\eta:\cale \to \cale’$, is a natural transformation of the underlying functors. In other words, such a morphism is a collection of functions

\begin{equation}\notag \eta_U: \cale(U) \to \cale’(U), \end{equation}

one for each open set $U\subseteq M$, that commute with the restriction maps of $\cale$ and $\cale’$.

The identity morphism

\begin{equation}\notag \id_\cale: \cale \to \cale \end{equation}

of the sheaf $\cale$ is defined by setting $(\id_{\cale})_U = \id_{\cale(U)}$ for each open set $U$.

If $\eta’: \cale’ \to \cale’’$ is a second morphism of sheaves, the composition

\begin{equation}\notag \eta’ \circ \eta: \cale \to \cale’’ \end{equation}

is defined by setting $(\eta’ \circ \eta)_U = \eta’_U \circ \eta_U$ for each open set $U$.

With sheaves of sets as objects, and with these morphisms, identity morphisms, and composition law, we obtain the category of sheaves on $X$, denoted $\Sh_X$.

There are multiple ways to transport sheaves from one topological space to another. One of the simplest is defined in:

Definition. Let $\cale$ be a sheaf of sets on a topological space $X$ and let $\alpha:X \to Y$ be a continuous map of topological spaces. We define the direct image of $\cale$ along $\alpha$, denoted $\alpha_\ast \cale$, to be sheaf in $\Sh_Y$ defined on sections by

\begin{equation}\notag (\alpha_\ast\cale)(U) = \cale(\alpha^{-1}(U)). \end{equation}

Exercise.

  1. Complete the definition of the direct image $\alpha_\ast \cale$ by describing its (obvious) restriction maps.
  2. Let $\alpha:(X,\sco_X) \to (Y,\sco_Y)$ be a morphism of ringed spaces. Recall that this means $\alpha:X \to Y$ is a continuous map such that for each open set $V$ in $Y$, the function \begin{equation}\notag \alpha^\ast_V: \sco_Y(V) \to \sco_X(\alpha^{-1}(V)), \quad f\mapsto f\circ \alpha, \end{equation} is a well defined homomorphism of $\bbr$-algebras. (Remember that the structure sheaves of our ringed spaces are assumed to be sheaves of $\bbr$-valued functions!) Prove that these pullbacks $\alpha^\ast_V$ assemble together to yield a morphism \begin{equation}\notag \alpha^\ast: \sco_Y \to \alpha_\ast \sco_X \end{equation} of sheaves over $Y$.

If $(X,\sco_X)$ is a locally ringed space, we defined the stalks $\sco_{X,p}$ of the structure sheaves in the previous post. This definition was the obvious adaptation of the definition of the local rings $C^\infty_{M,p}$ over a manifold $M$. In both cases, the structure sheaves were sheaves of actual functions (remember, we required $\sco_X$ to be a sheaf of functions!), and the germs in the stalks were certain equivalence classes of functions.

For a general sheaf $\cale$ on a topological space $X$, however, the sections in $\cale(U)$ may not be functions. Nevertheless, it is still possible to define stalks $\cale_p$ following essentially the same recipe.

Definition. Let $\cale$ be a sheaf of sets on a topological space $X$. Suppose we are given a point $p\in X$ and two sections $s_U\in \cale(U)$ and $s_V\in \cale(V)$, where $U$ and $V$ are open neighorhoods of $p$. We define $s_U$ and $s_V$ to be equivalent if there is an open neighborhood $W$ of $p$ contained in $U\cap V$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag r^U_{W}(s_U) = r^V_W(s_V), \end{equation}

where $r^U_W$ and $r^V_W$ are the restriction maps of the sheaf $\cale$. This defines an equivalence relation on the set

\begin{equation}\notag \{s_U \in \cale(U) : \text{$U$ an open neighborhood of $p$} \} \end{equation}

whose equivalence classes are called germs at $p$. We define the stalk of $\cale$ at $p$, denoted $\cale_p$, to be the set of all germs at $p$.

Exercise.

  1. Prove that the equivalence relation just described actually is an equivalence relation.
  2. Let $\eta: \cale \to \cale’$ be a morphism of sheaves of sets on $X$. For each $p\in X$, prove that the induced map \begin{equation}\notag \eta_p: \cale_p \to \cale’_p, \quad [s_U] \mapsto [\eta_U(s_U)], \end{equation} on stalks is well defined. Here, I am writing $[s_U]$ to denote the germ represented by a section $s_U \in \cale(U)$.

Note: Following our practice of intentionally confusing functions with germs in the local rings $C^\infty_{M,p}$ over a manifold $M$, in the future, when no confusion will likely arise, we will drop the bars from the notation $[s_U]$ and write $s_U$ for both the section and its germ.

Now, the main definition:

Definition. Let $(X,\sco_X)$ be a locally ringed space. An $\sco_X$-module is a sheaf of sets $\cale$ such that, for each open set $U\subseteq X$, the set of sections $\cale(U)$ is a module over the ring $\sco_X(U)$. Furthermore, if $U\subseteq V$ is a containment of open sets and

\begin{equation}\notag \rho^V_U: \sco_X(V) \to \sco_X(U) \quad \text{and} \quad r^V_U:\cale(V) \to \cale(U) \end{equation}

are the associated restriction maps, then we require $r^V_U$ to be $\sco_X(V)$-linear, where $\cale(U)$ is given a $\sco_X(V)$-module structure by restricting scalars along $\rho^V_U$.

A morphism $\eta:\cale \to \calf$ of the underlying sheaves of two $\sco_X$-modules is called $\sco_X$-linear if each function

\begin{equation}\notag \eta_U: \cale(U) \to \calf(U) \end{equation}

is $\sco_X(U)$-linear.

We define the identity morphism of an $\sco_X$-module to be its identity morphism as a sheaf in $\Sh_X$, and we define the composition of two $\sco_X$-linear morphisms to be their composition in $\Sh_X$.

With $\sco_X$-modules as objects, $\sco_X$-linear morphisms as morphisms, and with these identity morphisms and composition law, we obtain the category of $\sco_X$-modules, denoted $\Mod{\sco_X}$.

Exercise.

  1. We can easily give one obvious (but important!) example of an $\sco_X$-module: Convince yourself that the structure sheaf $\sco_X$ is an $\sco_X$-module.
  2. Prove that the stalk $\cale_p$ of an $\sco_X$-module is a $\sco_{X,p}$-module with respect to the evident operations. (If you’ve read through the previous two posts, you’ve done an exercise similar to this twice!)
  3. Let $\eta: \cale \to \cale’$ be an $\sco_X$-linear morphism. For each $p\in X$, prove that the induced map on stalks $\eta_p: \cale_p \to \cale’_{p}$ is $\sco_{X,p}$-linear.

We will show in the next section that the “local sections” of a vector bundle $E$ in $\Vect_M$ naturally form a $C^\infty_M$-module, where $C^\infty_M$ is the structure sheaf of the manifold $M$. However, the local triviality of the vector bundle is reflected as “local freeness” of the sheaf, which we need to define.

We require two preliminary definitions.

Definition. Let $\cale$ be a sheaf of sets on a topological space $X$. For an open set $U\subseteq X$, we define the restricted sheaf, denoted $\cale|_U$, to be the sheaf in $\Sh_U$ given by

\begin{equation}\notag \cale|_U(V) = \cale(V), \end{equation}

for all open sets $V\subseteq U$.

Exercise.

  1. Complete the definition of the restricted sheaf $\cale|_U$ by describing its (obvious) restriction maps.
  2. Suppose now that $(X,\sco_X)$ is a locally ringed space. For each open set $U\subseteq X$, show that $(U,\sco_U)$ is a locally ringed space, where $\sco_U$ is defined to be the restricted sheaf $\sco_X|_U$. Prove that the inclusion map $\iota:U \to X$ induces a morphism $\iota:(U,\sco_U) \to (X,\sco_X)$ of locally ringed spaces. Prove that the associated morphism of sheaves $\iota^\ast: \sco_X \to \iota_\ast \sco_U$ induces an isomorphism $\iota^\ast_p : \sco_{X,p} \to \sco_{U,p}$ of stalks as local $\bbr$-algebras.
  3. Building off of (2.), suppose that $\cale$ is a $\sco_X$-module. Show that the restricted sheaf $\cale|_U$ is an $\sco_U$-module over the locally ringed space $(U,\sco_U)$.
  4. Building off of (3.), for each $p\in U$, prove that the stalks $(\cale|_{U})_p$ and $\cale_p$ are naturally isomorphic as $\sco_{X,p}$-modules, where the first stalk is given an $\sco_{X,p}$-module structure by restricting scalars along the isomorphism $\iota^\ast_p$.

The second preliminary definition is:

Definition. Let $\cale$ and $\cale’$ be two $\sco_X$-modules over a locally ringed space $(X,\sco_X)$. We define their direct sum, denoted $\cale \oplus \cale’$, to be the sheaf defined on sections by

\begin{equation}\notag (\cale \oplus \cale’)(U) = \cale(U) \oplus \cale’(U). \end{equation}

Exercise.

  1. Complete the definition of the direct sum $\cale \oplus \cale’$ as a sheaf in $\Sh_X$ by describing its (obvious) restriction maps. Then, show that $\cale \oplus \cale’$ is indeed a $\sco_X$-module.
  2. Show that restriction of $\sco_X$-modules to open sets commutes with direct sums. That is, for two $\sco_X$-modules $\cale$ and $\cale’$ and an open set $U\subseteq X$, show that \begin{equation}\notag (\cale \oplus \cale’)|_U = \cale|_U \oplus \cale’|_U \end{equation} as $\sco_U$-modules.
  3. Prove that the natural maps on sections \begin{equation}\notag \cale(U) \leftrightarrows \cale(U) \oplus \cale’(U) \rightleftarrows \cale’(U) \end{equation} assemble together to yield well-defined $\sco_X$-linear morphisms \begin{equation}\notag \cale \leftrightarrows \cale \oplus \cale’ \rightleftarrows \cale’ \end{equation}
  4. For each $p\in X$, prove that the natural maps in (3.) induce an $\sco_{X,p}$-linear isomorphism \begin{equation}\notag (\cale \oplus \cale’)_p \cong \cale_p \oplus \cale’_p. \end{equation}
  5. Generalize the definition of binary direct sums to the case of a direct sum \begin{equation}\notag \cale_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus \cale_k \end{equation} of an arbitrary (finite) number of $\sco_X$-modules. In particular, show that each direct sum \begin{equation}\notag \sco_X^k \defeq \underbrace{\sco_X \oplus \cdots \oplus \sco_X}_{\text{$k$ summands}} \end{equation} is an $\sco_X$-module.

Now, the main definition of this section.

Definition. Let $\cale$ be an $\sco_X$-module over a locally ringed space $(X,\sco_X)$.

  1. We shall say $\cale$ is free of rank $k$ if it is isomorphic in $\Mod{\sco_X}$ to a direct sum of the form $\sco_X^k$.
  2. We shall say $\cale$ is locally free of rank $k$ if $X$ has an open cover $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$ such that $\cale|_{U_i}$ is free of rank $k$ in $\Mod{\sco_{U_i}}$. In other words, we require that for each $i\in I$ there exists an $\sco_{U_i}$-linear isomorphism \begin{equation}\label{table-eqn} \Psi_i: \sco_{U_i}^k \to \cale|_{U_i} \end{equation} where $\sco_{U_i} = \sco_X|_{U_i}$. In this case, the open over $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$ is called a trivializing open cover of the module, the module is said to trivialize over $\{U_i\}_{i\in I}$, and isomorphisms \eqref{table-eqn} are called (local) trivializations.

Sections of vector bundles

In this section we study smooth sections of (locally trivial) vector bundles. We have already seen examples of these gadgets in two places: (1) One guise of smooth vector fields on a manifold is as smooth (global) sections of the tangent bundle, and (2) smooth sections of the trivial vector bundle $M \times \bbr \to M$ are exactly smooth functions on $M$. Indeed, we covered (1) in this post, while to verify that claim (2) is true, we note that a smooth map $\sigma: M \to M \times \bbr$ of the form

\begin{equation}\notag \sigma(p) = (p,f(p)) \end{equation}

is uniquely determined by the smooth function $f\in C^\infty(M)$. In general, the definition is as follows:

Definition. Let $\pi:E \to M$ be a vector bundle and $U\subseteq M$ a nonempty open set. A local section of the vector bundle over $U$ is a function $\sigma:U\to E$ such that $\pi\circ \sigma = \id_U$. We shall say $\sigma$ is a smooth local section if it is a smooth map between manifolds.

We write $\Gamma(U,E)$ for the set of smooth local sections over $U$. If the vector bundle $E$ is clear from context, we will write $\Gamma(U)$ in place of $\Gamma(U,E)$. Sections in $\Gamma(M)$ are called smooth global sections.

We will often write the functional value $\sigma(p) \in E_p$ of a section as $\sigma_p$. If the section happens to have an index, like $\sigma_i$, then we will write $\sigma_i|_p$ in place of $(\sigma_i)_p$.

Exercise. Given two smooth local sections $\sigma,\tau\in \Gamma(U)$, we define their sum, denoted $\sigma+\tau$, via the formula

\begin{equation}\notag (\sigma+\tau)_p = \sigma_p + \tau_p, \end{equation}

where the sum on the right-hand side occurs in the fiber $E_p$. If $f\in C^\infty(U)$ is a function, we define the product of $f$ and $\sigma$, denoted $f\sigma$, via the formula

\begin{equation}\notag (f\sigma)_p = f(p) \sigma_p, \end{equation}

where the product on the right-hand side occurs in the fiber $E_p$. Prove that with respect to these operations, the set $\Gamma(U)$ is a $C^\infty(U)$-module.

A smoothly varying collection of bases of the fibers of a vector bundle is called a smooth local frame, which is terminology that you should recognize from the earlier post on vector fields. Officially:

Definition. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ and let $U\subseteq M$ be a nonempty open set. Then a collection of smooth local sections $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k \in \Gamma(U)$ is called a smooth local frame over $U$ if

\begin{equation}\notag \vartheta_1|_p,\ldots,\vartheta_k|_p \in E_p \end{equation}

is a basis for all $p\in U$.

One of the main uses of smooth local frames is to test for smoothness of general sections:

Theorem (Componentwise criterion for smoothness). Let $\pi:E\to M$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ and let $U\subseteq M$ be a nonempty open set. Given a function $\sigma:U\to E$ (not necessarily smooth, or even continuous) such that $\pi \circ \sigma = \id_U$ and a smooth local frame $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k\in \Gamma(U)$, we may write

\begin{equation}\notag \sigma_p = \sigma^i(p) \vartheta_i|_p \end{equation}

for each $p\in U$, where $\sigma^i: U \to \bbr$ are uniquely determined real-valued functions (not necessarily smooth, or even continuous). These functions are called the component functions of $\sigma$ with respect to the local frame.

Then $\sigma$ is smooth if and only if each component function $\sigma^i$ is smooth.

Exercise. Prove that the local frame $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k \in \Gamma(U)$ forms a basis as a $C^\infty(U)$-module.

Given a manifold $M$, we recall that we proved the assignments $U \mapsto C^\infty(U)$ and $U \mapsto \calx(U)$ are examples of sheaves in this post. Had we known at the time that $\calx(U)$ could be viewed as smooth local sections of a certain vector bundle, then we could have applied the following general result:

Theorem (Sections of vector bundles form sheaves). Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$. If for each open set $U\subseteq M$ we define $\cale(U) = \Gamma(U,E)$, then $\cale$ is a $C^\infty_M$-module, where $C^\infty_M$ is the sheaf of smooth functions on $M$. The sheaf $\cale$ is called the sheaf of sections of the vector bundle $E$.

We learned the general definition of a sheaf in the previous post. When we say that $\cale$ is a sheaf of $C^\infty_M$-modules, we really mean that $\cale$ is a sheaf of modules over the sheaf of rings $C^\infty_M$. For the general definition of these latter types of objects, see here.

You also learned the definition of stalks of structure sheaves of ringed spaces in the previous post. It turns out that such stalks may be defined for any sheaf and, in particular, they may be defined for sheaves of sections of vector bundles. You should have no trouble, therefore, in adapting the definition of stalks in the previous post to apply to our sheaves of sections. Otherwise, see here for the official definition.

Let’s set some notation for stalks:

Definition. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. We will write $\cale_p$ for the stalk of $\cale$ at $p\in M$. Elements in $\cale_p$ will be called germs of sections.

Exercise.

  1. Prove that each stalk $\cale_p$ is a module over the local ring $C^\infty_{M,p}$ with respect the evident operations. (By now, I’m hoping that this proof is routine!)
  2. For each $p\in M$, recall that there is a well-defined homomorphism \begin{equation}\notag \dev_p:C^\infty_{M,p} \to \bbr, \quad f \mapsto f(p), \end{equation} of $\bbr$-algebras. By restricting scalars along this map, a fiber $E_p$ of the vector bundle may be given a $C^\infty_{M,p}$-module structure. With respect to this structure, prove that the map \begin{equation}\notag e_p: \cale_p \to E_p, \quad \sigma \mapsto \sigma_p, \end{equation} is a well-defined homomorphism of $C^\infty_{M,p}$-modules.

Remember that the germs in the stalk $\cale_p$ are actually equivalence classes of sections $\sigma$ defined on open neighborhoods of $p$, so my definition “$\sigma \mapsto \sigma_p$” of the evaluation map $e_p$ is technically an abuse of notation. However, as I’ve mentioned in the past, this is a typical abuse, and going forth I will use it without further comment.

Take care to notice the difference between $\cale_p$ and $E_p$ for a vector bundle $E$ over a manifold $M$. The former is the stalk of the sheaf of sections at the point $p\in M$, while the latter is the fiber over $p$. While these are different objects, there is, nevertheless, a tight link between them:

Theorem. Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. For each $p\in M$, let $\mfm_p$ denote the maximal ideal of the local ring $C^\infty_{M,p}$. The sequence

\begin{equation}\notag 0 \to \mfm_p \to \cale_p \xrightarrow{e_p} E_p \to 0 \end{equation}

is exact, where the first map is the inclusion and the second is the evaluation map.

A proof is in contained in Jet’s book, Chapter 12.

Theorem (Extension theorem for local sections). Let $E$ be a vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. Then for each $p\in M$ and each open neighbohrood $U$ of $p$, the natural localization map

\begin{equation}\notag \lambda_{U,p}: \cale(U) \to \cale_p \end{equation}

sending a section to the germ that it represents is surjective.

The proof uses bump functions, just like the same extension theorem for smooth functions.

Theorem (Stalks of vector bundles are free). Let $E$ be a rank-$k$ vector bundle over a manifold $M$ with sheaf of sections $\cale$. Then for each $p\in M$, the stalk $\cale_p$ is a free $C^\infty_{M,p}$-module of rank $k$. In particular, if $U$ is an open neighborhood of $p$ over which $E$ trivializes, then the natural map

\begin{equation}\label{nat-eqn} C^\infty_{M,p} \otimes_{C^\infty(U)} \cale(U) \to \cale_p, \quad f \otimes \sigma \mapsto f\lambda_{U,p}(\sigma), \end{equation}

is an isomorphism of $C^\infty_{M,p}$-modules, where $\lambda_{U,p}$ is the natural localization map from the previous theorem.

We have already observed that a choice of local frame

\begin{equation}\notag \vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k \in \cale(U) \end{equation}

forms a basis of $\cale(U)$ as a $C^\infty(U)$-module, and that the localization map $\lambda_{U,p}$ is surjective. Thus, we need only show that the map \eqref{nat-eqn} is injective. So, suppose that we have an element $f^i \otimes \vartheta_i$ in the domain of \eqref{nat-eqn}, with summation over repeated indices implied. Suppose that it is sent to zero in the stalk, so that we have $f^i \vartheta_i = 0$. (Be very careful to understand exactly what this equation means, as I am confusing sections with germs again, and have omitted the localization map.) By shrinking $U$ to a smaller neighborhood $V$ of $p$ if needed, we may suppose that this equation actually occurs in the module $\cale(V)$, where now $f^i \in C^\infty(V)$. But the sections $\vartheta_1,\ldots,\vartheta_k$ are linearly independent in $\cale(V)$ as well, and hence $f^i = 0$ on $V$ for each $i$. Hence $f^i=0$ in $C^\infty_{M,p}$, which is what we wanted to prove. Q.E.D.

Constructing vector bundles

A fundamental equivalence of categories
